subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: FIPPA — Right of Access; Mandatory vs Discretionary Exemptions
fact_pattern: Beatrice Fontaine is a policy researcher at a Toronto-based think tank who lives in Québec. She submits a FIPPA request to the Ontario Ministry of Health for two categories of records: (1) a ministerial briefing note prepared by Ministry lawyers advising the Minister on potential litigation exposure arising from a delayed drug-approval decision; and (2) records identifying by name the individual patients who filed complaints about the drug-approval delay. The Ministry's access coordinator reviews the request and, without citing any provision or explaining any exercise of discretion, sends a single-line refusal stating: "The requested records are exempt under FIPPA."
call_of_question: Which of the following best describes the legal errors, if any, in the Ministry's refusal?

options.A: The refusal is valid in its entirety because Beatrice, as a Québec resident rather than an Ontario resident, has no right of access under FIPPA, and the Ministry was correct to deny the request without further analysis.
options.B: The refusal is defective because the Ministry failed to distinguish between a mandatory exemption (s. 21 personal privacy for the patient records) and a discretionary exemption (s. 19 solicitor-client privilege for the legal briefing note); for the s. 19 exemption the Ministry was required to affirmatively exercise and justify its discretion to withhold, which it did not do.
options.C: The refusal is valid for both categories of records because once any statutory exemption potentially applies to a record, the institution has fulfilled its FIPPA obligations by issuing a blanket refusal without identifying the specific exemption relied upon.
options.D: The refusal is defective only as to the patient records because s. 21 personal privacy is a mandatory exemption that the Ministry must apply automatically regardless of the circumstances; the legal briefing note is exempt under s. 19 and no further analysis or exercise of discretion is required for solicitor-client privileged records.

explanation: Under FIPPA s. 10, every person — without restriction to Ontario residents — has a right of access to a record in the custody or under the control of an institution. The burden is on the institution to demonstrate that an exemption applies. FIPPA draws a critical distinction between mandatory exemptions (using 'shall refuse' — e.g., s. 21 personal privacy) and discretionary exemptions (using 'may refuse' — e.g., s. 19 solicitor-client privilege, s. 14 law enforcement). For discretionary exemptions, the institution must affirmatively decide to exercise its discretion to withhold and must be able to demonstrate that it did so; a blanket refusal with no identification of the exemption claimed and no exercise of discretion is procedurally defective. Here, the s. 21 exemption for patient names would be mandatory (the Ministry shall withhold), but the s. 19 exemption for the legal briefing note is discretionary — the Ministry must identify the exemption, confirm the record qualifies, and show it exercised its discretion to claim the exemption. Option A is wrong because FIPPA s. 10 grants access to 'every person,' not merely Ontario residents. Option C is wrong because a blanket refusal without specifying the exemption and without exercising discretion for discretionary exemptions violates the Act's procedural requirements. Option D incorrectly characterizes the s. 19 exemption — solicitor-client privilege under FIPPA is discretionary, not mandatory, and the Ministry must exercise its discretion; the statement that 'no further analysis or exercise of discretion is required' is the critical error.
why_A_wrong: Overstatement Trap: FIPPA s. 10 grants a right of access to 'every person' — there is no residency requirement. Beatrice's Québec residence does not disqualify her from submitting a FIPPA request to an Ontario institution.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Ministry's blanket refusal was procedurally defective: for the s. 19 solicitor-client privilege exemption (discretionary), it was required to identify the exemption and demonstrate that it exercised its discretion to withhold; it did neither.
why_C_wrong: General Rule Trap: while institutions may rely on exemptions, a blanket refusal citing no specific provision and exercising no discretion does not satisfy FIPPA's procedural requirements. The institution bears the burden of demonstrating the exemption applies and, for discretionary exemptions, of showing it exercised its discretion.
why_D_wrong: Exception Trap: s. 19 solicitor-client privilege under FIPPA is a discretionary exemption — the institution 'may' refuse, not 'shall' refuse. The statement that no exercise of discretion is required for s. 19 records reverses the mandatory/discretionary distinction and is the core error in Option D.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: FIPPA — Right of Access; Mandatory vs Discretionary Exemptions
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 10, s. 14, s. 19, s. 21

tested_concepts: FIPPA s. 10 — right of access for every person regardless of residency
mandatory vs discretionary FIPPA exemptions
s. 19 solicitor-client privilege as a discretionary exemption
s. 21 personal privacy as a mandatory exemption
institution's burden to identify exemption and exercise discretion
procedural deficiency in blanket refusal

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: FIPPA Exemptions — Section 14 Law Enforcement and Section 19 Solicitor-Client Privilege
fact_pattern: A requester submits a FIPPA access request to the Ontario Provincial Police for records relating to an ongoing criminal investigation. The OPP refuses access, citing s. 14 (law enforcement exemption). The requester then submits a separate request to the Ministry of the Attorney General for legal memoranda prepared by Ministry lawyers advising the government on the same investigation. The Ministry refuses access, citing s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege).
call_of_question: Which of the following correctly distinguishes the s. 14 law enforcement exemption from the s. 19 solicitor-client privilege exemption under FIPPA?

options.A: Both s. 14 and s. 19 are mandatory exemptions; the institution must withhold records in both cases without any exercise of discretion.
options.B: Section 14 is a discretionary exemption that the institution may claim for records that could reasonably be expected to interfere with an ongoing law enforcement investigation; s. 19 is also a discretionary exemption protecting records that are subject to solicitor-client privilege, but the institution may choose to waive the privilege and disclose.
options.C: Section 14 is a mandatory exemption; s. 19 is a discretionary exemption that the institution may choose to claim or waive.
options.D: Section 19 is a mandatory exemption; s. 14 is a discretionary exemption requiring the institution to exercise its discretion to withhold.

explanation: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, both s. 14 (law enforcement) and s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege) are discretionary exemptions — the institution 'may' refuse access rather than 'shall' refuse access. For s. 14, the institution may refuse access to records where disclosure could reasonably be expected to interfere with law enforcement matters. For s. 19, the institution may refuse access to records subject to solicitor-client privilege. Because both are discretionary, the institution must exercise its discretion whether to claim the exemption; the institution may choose to disclose even if the exemption technically applies.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Section 14 and s. 19 are discretionary exemptions under FIPPA; the word 'may' in these provisions signals discretionary, not mandatory, application. Compare s. 21 (personal privacy), which uses 'shall.'
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 14 is a discretionary exemption that the institution may claim for records that could reasonably be expected to interfere with an ongoing law enforcement investigation; s. 19 is also a discretionary exemption protecting records that are subject to solicitor-client privilege, but the institution may choose to waive the privilege and disclose.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Section 14 is not mandatory — it uses the word 'may,' not 'shall,' and is therefore discretionary.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 19 is not mandatory; like s. 14, it uses the word 'may' and is a discretionary exemption.

source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: FIPPA Exemptions — Section 14 Law Enforcement and Section 19 Solicitor-Client Privilege
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 14 (law enforcement), s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege)

FIPPA s. 19 solicitor-client privilege discretionary exemption
may vs shall distinction mandatory vs discretionary

subject: public_law
fact_pattern: A person submits a FIPPA request to the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services for records relating to a foster care placement decision that affected her family. The records contain personal information about the biological parents of the child placed in care. The Ministry refuses access under s. 21 (personal privacy exemption). The requester argues that disclosure is necessary to allow her to understand the basis for a decision that directly affected her.
call_of_question: Under FIPPA, is s. 21 (personal privacy) a mandatory or discretionary exemption, and does it have any exceptions that might allow disclosure?

options.A: Section 21 is a discretionary exemption; the Ministry may choose to disclose personal information in any case where it considers disclosure in the public interest.
options.B: Section 21 is a mandatory exemption — the Ministry shall refuse to disclose personal information about a third party; however, FIPPA provides defined exceptions including where disclosure is desirable for the purpose of subjecting the activities of the Government to public scrutiny, or where the individual to whom the information relates has consented.
options.C: Section 21 is a mandatory exemption with no exceptions; personal information about a third party can never be disclosed under FIPPA.
options.D: Section 21 is not an exemption at all; it simply requires the Ministry to notify the third party before making a disclosure decision.

explanation: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 21(1), an institution shall refuse to disclose personal information to any person other than the individual to whom the information relates — making it a mandatory exemption (using 'shall'). However, s. 21(1) is subject to a number of defined exceptions, including: where the person to whom the information relates has consented (s. 21(1)(a)); where disclosure is desirable for the purpose of subjecting the activities of the Government to public scrutiny (s. 21(1)(f)(iii)); and other listed exceptions. The institution must consider whether any exception applies before invoking the mandatory exemption.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Section 21 uses the word 'shall' and is a mandatory exemption; the institution does not have general discretion to disclose personal information whenever it considers it in the public interest.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 21 is a mandatory exemption — the Ministry shall refuse to disclose personal information about a third party; however, FIPPA provides defined exceptions including where disclosure is desirable for the purpose of subjecting the activities of the Government to public scrutiny, or where the individual to whom the information relates has consented.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. While s. 21 is mandatory, it has enumerated exceptions; personal information is not absolutely protected with no exceptions whatsoever.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 21 is a substantive exemption requiring the institution to refuse disclosure of personal information, not merely a notice provision.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: FIPPA Exemptions — Section 21 Personal Privacy
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 21(1)

tested_concepts: FIPPA s. 21 mandatory personal privacy exemption
shall vs may mandatory vs discretionary
exceptions to s. 21 personal privacy

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: PIPEDA — Application, Consent Principles, and Federal Jurisdiction
fact_pattern: A national retail chain operating across Canada collects customer purchase data and shares it with a third-party marketing company without the customers' knowledge or consent. Customers complain that their personal information has been shared without authorization. They are advised that Ontario's FIPPA does not apply because the retailer is a private sector company.
call_of_question: Which privacy statute governs the collection and disclosure of personal information by private sector organizations carrying on commercial activities in Canada, and what is the foundational consent principle?

options.A: Ontario's Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA) applies to all private sector organizations operating in Ontario.
options.B: There is no federal privacy legislation governing the private sector; privacy protection for private sector organizations is entirely a matter of provincial jurisdiction.
options.C: The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), S.C. 2000, c. 5, applies to organizations that collect, use, or disclose personal information in the course of commercial activities; PIPEDA's foundational principle requires organizations to obtain meaningful consent from individuals before collecting, using, or disclosing their personal information.
options.D: FIPPA applies to both public institutions and private companies operating in Ontario where the company performs government-like functions.

explanation: Under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5 (PIPEDA), is the federal statute governing the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information by private sector organizations in the course of commercial activities. PIPEDA applies in provinces that have not enacted substantially similar privacy legislation (Alberta, British Columbia, and Quebec have their own legislation). PIPEDA's Schedule 1 sets out ten fair information principles based on the Canadian Standards Association Model Code, with consent as the foundational principle: organizations must obtain the knowledge and consent of the individual for any collection, use, or disclosure of personal information unless an exception applies.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. MFIPPA applies to Ontario municipalities and their boards and agencies; it does not govern private sector organizations.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. PIPEDA is a federal statute that governs the private sector in the course of commercial activities; Parliament has jurisdiction over the private sector under its trade and commerce power.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), S.C. 2000, c. 5, applies to organizations that collect, use, or disclose personal information in the course of commercial activities; PIPEDA's foundational principle requires organizations to obtain meaningful consent from individuals before collecting, using, or disclosing their personal information.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. FIPPA applies to Ontario public institutions; it does not apply to private companies simply because they perform government-like functions.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: PIPEDA — Application, Consent Principles, and Federal Jurisdiction
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5 (PIPEDA), Schedule 1

tested_concepts: PIPEDA application to private sector commercial activities
PIPEDA consent as foundational principle
distinction from FIPPA and MFIPPA

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario
subtopic_heading: IPC — Orders, Mediation, and Appeal Route
fact_pattern: A requester submits a FIPPA access request to the Ontario Ministry of Finance. The Ministry refuses access under s. 19 (solicitor-client privilege). The requester disagrees and wishes to challenge the refusal. The requester's lawyer advises that the dispute should first go to the Information and Privacy Commissioner before any court proceedings.
call_of_question: Under FIPPA, what is the role of the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario in reviewing a disputed access refusal, and what is the appeal route after an IPC order?

options.A: The IPC acts as a mediator only; it has no power to issue binding orders and any order it makes may be ignored by the institution.
options.B: Under FIPPA, a requester who has been refused access may appeal the refusal to the IPC; the IPC investigates and may mediate the dispute. If mediation fails, the IPC may conduct an inquiry and issue a binding order requiring disclosure or confirming the exemption; the order may be appealed to the Divisional Court.
options.C: The IPC's role is limited to recommending changes to government privacy practices; it cannot intervene in individual access disputes.
options.D: A requester must bring a judicial review application directly to the Divisional Court without first going to the IPC.

explanation: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, a requester who is refused access may appeal the refusal to the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario. The IPC has a multi-stage process: it may attempt to resolve the dispute through mediation, and if mediation fails, may conduct a formal inquiry. Following an inquiry, the IPC has the power to issue binding orders — including orders requiring the institution to disclose records — or confirming the institution's right to withhold. Orders of the IPC may be appealed to the Divisional Court under s. 54 of FIPPA.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The IPC has the power to issue binding orders; its orders are not merely advisory and institutions must comply with them or appeal to the Divisional Court.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under FIPPA, a requester who has been refused access may appeal the refusal to the IPC; the IPC investigates and may mediate the dispute. If mediation fails, the IPC may conduct an inquiry and issue a binding order requiring disclosure or confirming the exemption; the order may be appealed to the Divisional Court.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The IPC has both systemic oversight functions and the power to adjudicate individual access disputes; it can intervene in individual cases and issue binding orders.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. A requester must exhaust the IPC process before seeking judicial review; the Divisional Court's role is to review orders of the IPC, not to hear original access disputes.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: IPC — Orders, Mediation, and Appeal Route
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 54 (appeal to Divisional Court)

IPC binding orders
appeal from IPC to Divisional Court s. 54

subject: public_law
fact_pattern: A construction company submits a bid on a provincial government contract. A competitor submits a FIPPA access request for all documents related to the successful bid, including the winning company's pricing breakdown, proprietary cost estimates, and project methodology. The government institution has notified the winning company of the request. The winning company opposes disclosure, arguing the information is commercial and was provided in confidence.
call_of_question: Under s. 17 of FIPPA, what test must be satisfied for a third party to successfully claim the third-party information exemption?

options.A: Any information provided by a private party to a government institution is automatically exempt from disclosure; no further test is required.
options.B: Under FIPPA s. 17, the third-party information exemption applies where disclosure of the record could reasonably be expected to: (1) prejudice significantly the competitive position of the third party; (2) interfere with contractual or other negotiations; (3) result in similar information no longer being supplied to the institution; or the information was supplied in confidence and was treated as confidential by both parties, and disclosure could result in material financial loss to the third party.
options.C: Section 17 applies only to government records relating to pharmaceutical companies; it has no application to construction industry records.
options.D: Section 17 is a mandatory exemption that requires refusal of access to all records submitted by private parties to government institutions, without any further analysis.

explanation: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 17(1), an institution shall refuse to disclose a record that reveals a trade secret or commercial, financial, labour relations, scientific, or technical information supplied in confidence to the institution by a third party, where the disclosure could reasonably be expected to: (a) prejudice significantly the competitive position or interfere with the contractual or other negotiations of the third party; (b) result in similar information no longer being supplied to the institution; or (c) result in undue loss or gain to any person, group, committee, or financial institution or agency. The third party must establish all elements: the information is commercial in nature, was supplied in confidence, and disclosure would cause the described harm.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Not all information provided by private parties to government institutions is automatically exempt; the third party must establish the specific conditions set out in s. 17(1), including that the information was supplied in confidence and that defined harms would result from disclosure.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under FIPPA s. 17, the third-party information exemption applies where disclosure of the record could reasonably be expected to: (1) prejudice significantly the competitive position of the third party; (2) interfere with contractual or other negotiations; (3) result in similar information no longer being supplied to the institution; or the information was supplied in confidence and was treated as confidential by both parties, and disclosure could result in material financial loss to the third party.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Section 17 applies broadly to commercial, financial, labour relations, scientific, or technical information supplied by any third party; it is not limited to pharmaceutical companies or any specific industry.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 17 is a mandatory exemption that applies where the conditions are satisfied, but the institution must conduct a contextual analysis to determine whether the specific conditions in s. 17(1) are met; it does not automatically apply to all records submitted by private parties.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: FIPPA Section 17 — Third-Party Commercial Information
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 17(1)

commercial information supplied in confidence
harm to competitive position as required element

subject: public_law
topic_heading: FIPPA — Records in Custody or Control
subtopic_heading: FIPPA — What Constitutes a Record in the Custody or Control of an Institution
fact_pattern: A journalist submits a FIPPA request to the Ontario Ministry of Infrastructure for all emails and text messages sent on the personal cell phones of three senior Ministry officials relating to a major infrastructure contract. The Ministry responds that personal cell phones are not Ministry property and that personal device communications are outside its custody and control. The journalist argues the communications relate to Ministry business and should be disclosed.
call_of_question: Under FIPPA, when are records held on personal devices of government officials in the custody or control of the institution for the purpose of the right of access?

options.A: Records are in the custody or control of the institution only if they are physically stored on government servers or equipment; personal device communications can never be subject to FIPPA access requests.
options.B: Under FIPPA, 'custody or control' is determined by examining the nature of the record's content and the purpose for which it was created; records on personal devices that relate to the conduct of government business may be in the control of the institution for FIPPA purposes, even if they are not physically stored on government equipment.
options.C: All communications on personal devices by government officials are automatically subject to FIPPA regardless of their content.
options.D: FIPPA applies only to records created during working hours on government premises; personal device communications outside office hours are never subject to FIPPA.

explanation: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 10, the right of access applies to records in the custody or control of an institution. The Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario has interpreted 'custody or control' broadly to include records in the physical possession of the institution and records over which the institution has the legal right to obtain. Records on personal devices may be in the institution's 'control' if they relate to government business and the institution has the authority and practical ability to obtain them. The test is functional and content-focused, not limited to physical storage on government equipment.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. 'Custody or control' under FIPPA is not limited to physical government servers or equipment; the IPC has interpreted control to include records the institution has a right to obtain, including content-based analysis of personal device records relating to government business.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under FIPPA, 'custody or control' is determined by examining the nature of the record's content and the purpose for which it was created; records on personal devices that relate to the conduct of government business may be in the control of the institution for FIPPA purposes, even if they are not physically stored on government equipment.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Not all communications on personal devices by government officials are automatically subject to FIPPA; the determination depends on whether the content relates to the conduct of government business and whether the institution has control over the records.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. FIPPA's definition of records is not limited to communications created during working hours on government premises; the focus is on the content and purpose of the record, not when or where it was created.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: FIPPA — What Constitutes a Record in the Custody or Control of an Institution
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 10; s. 2(1) (definition of record)

tested_concepts: FIPPA custody or control functional definition
personal device records relating to government business
not limited to physical government storage

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: PIPEDA — Exceptions to the Consent Requirement
fact_pattern: A bank detects unusual activity in a customer's account that suggests possible fraud. Without the customer's consent, the bank shares the customer's account transaction data with the Royal Canadian Mounted Police as part of a fraud investigation. The customer later learns of the disclosure and argues the bank violated PIPEDA by disclosing his personal information without consent.
call_of_question: Under PIPEDA, does the disclosure of personal information to law enforcement without the individual's consent constitute a violation of PIPEDA's consent principle?

options.A: Yes. PIPEDA's consent requirement is absolute; no disclosure of personal information to any third party, including law enforcement, is permitted without the individual's consent.
options.B: No. Under PIPEDA s. 7(3), an organization may disclose personal information without consent where the disclosure is to a government institution that has made a request and identified its lawful authority to obtain the information, or where the organization has reasonable grounds to believe the information relates to a breach of law.
options.C: Yes. The bank violated PIPEDA because only court-ordered production orders can authorize disclosure to law enforcement without consent.
options.D: No. Banks are exempt from all PIPEDA requirements because the Bank Act governs all aspects of banking including privacy.

explanation: Under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, Schedule 1 establishes the consent principle as foundational to PIPEDA. However, s. 7(3) of PIPEDA provides a number of exceptions to the requirement for consent for disclosure. An organization may disclose personal information without consent to a government institution that has identified its lawful authority to obtain the information and indicated that disclosure is requested for a specified purpose (s. 7(3)(c)). Additionally, s. 7(3)(d) permits disclosure without consent where the organization reasonably believes the information could be useful in investigating a contravention of federal or provincial law. The bank's disclosure to the RCMP in the context of a fraud investigation falls within these exceptions.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. PIPEDA's consent requirement is subject to enumerated exceptions in s. 7(3); the consent principle is foundational but not absolute. Law enforcement disclosures in defined circumstances do not require consent.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No. Under PIPEDA s. 7(3), an organization may disclose personal information without consent where the disclosure is to a government institution that has made a request and identified its lawful authority to obtain the information, or where the organization has reasonable grounds to believe the information relates to a breach of law.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. PIPEDA s. 7(3) permits voluntary disclosure to law enforcement without a court order in defined circumstances; a production order is not the only lawful mechanism for disclosure to law enforcement under PIPEDA.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Banks are subject to PIPEDA for their commercial activities involving personal information; the Bank Act does not exempt them from PIPEDA's privacy requirements.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: PIPEDA — Exceptions to the Consent Requirement
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, s. 7(3)(c), s. 7(3)(d)

tested_concepts: PIPEDA s. 7(3) consent exceptions
law enforcement disclosure exception
consent principle not absolute

subject: public_law
fact_pattern: ShopNation Inc. is a large e-commerce company that processes online retail transactions for approximately 2 million registered customers across Canada. On March 15, a hacker accessed ShopNation's customer database through an unpatched server vulnerability, extracting names, home addresses, credit card numbers, and purchase histories for 50,000 customers. ShopNation's Chief Privacy Officer conducts an internal assessment and determines that the breach creates a 'real risk of significant harm' to the affected individuals, including risks of identity theft and financial fraud. The privacy officer's legal counsel now advises on the company's mandatory obligations under PIPEDA. The company's CEO asks: 'Can we just notify the Privacy Commissioner and let the Commissioner decide whether customers need to be told? That way we avoid the reputational hit of direct customer notification if the Commissioner doesn't think it's warranted.'
call_of_question: The CEO's proposed approach — notifying the Privacy Commissioner only and leaving the decision on individual notification to the Commissioner — is being evaluated. Under PIPEDA, which of the following best describes the company's actual notification obligations?

options.A: The CEO's proposed approach is permissible. Where an organization notifies the Privacy Commissioner of Canada as soon as feasible, PIPEDA allows the Commissioner to assess the breach and determine whether individual notification is required; the organization's notification duty to affected individuals is conditional on the Commissioner's direction.
options.B: Under PIPEDA ss. 10.1 and 10.2 (as amended by the Digital Privacy Act, 2015), where a breach of security safeguards creates a real risk of significant harm, the organization must independently fulfill two simultaneous notification duties: (1) notify the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada as soon as feasible; and (2) notify affected individuals directly and as soon as feasible — regardless of whether the Commissioner has assessed the breach or directed notification. The organization must also maintain breach records.
options.C: The organization must notify only the affected individuals directly; notification to the Privacy Commissioner of Canada is optional at the organization's discretion unless the Commissioner affirmatively requests a report.
options.D: The mandatory notification obligation under PIPEDA is triggered only by breaches involving financial information; a breach of home addresses and purchase histories, without confirmed fraudulent use, does not create a 'real risk of significant harm' and does not trigger mandatory notification.

explanation: Under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, ss. 10.1 and 10.2 (in force since November 1, 2018), organizations are subject to two independent and simultaneous mandatory notification obligations when a breach creates a real risk of significant harm: (1) report to the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada as soon as feasible; and (2) directly notify the affected individuals as soon as feasible. The CEO's proposed approach — notifying only the Commissioner and delegating the individual notification decision to the Commissioner — misunderstands the statutory scheme. PIPEDA imposes both notification duties directly on the organization; neither obligation is conditional on the Commissioner's prior assessment, direction, or approval. The Commissioner's role is oversight and enforcement, not as an intermediary through whom individual notification is channelled. The organization must also maintain records of all breaches of security safeguards, even those that do not meet the 'real risk' threshold, and make those records available to the Commissioner on request.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. This is a General Rule Trap that mischaracterizes the Commissioner's statutory role. Under PIPEDA, the Commissioner does not serve as a gatekeeper who decides whether individual notification is required. The organization's duty to notify affected individuals is direct, mandatory, and operates simultaneously with the duty to notify the Commissioner. Neither duty is conditional on the other.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under PIPEDA ss. 10.1 and 10.2 (as amended by the Digital Privacy Act, 2015), where a breach of security safeguards creates a real risk of significant harm, the organization must independently fulfill two simultaneous notification duties: (1) notify the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada as soon as feasible; and (2) notify affected individuals directly and as soon as feasible — regardless of whether the Commissioner has assessed the breach or directed notification. The organization must also maintain breach records.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Notification to the Privacy Commissioner of Canada is a mandatory independent obligation — not optional. PIPEDA ss. 10.1 and 10.2 impose both the Commissioner notification duty and the individual notification duty; neither is optional and neither is subordinate to the other.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. This is a Wrong Timeline / Threshold Trap. The mandatory notification obligation is triggered by 'real risk of significant harm,' which is defined broadly to include bodily harm, humiliation, damage to reputation, financial loss, identity theft, and adverse effects on employment or other relationships. A breach involving home addresses and credit card numbers for 50,000 individuals creates clear risks of identity theft and financial fraud — the officer has already determined this. The obligation is not limited to situations where fraudulent use has been confirmed.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information
source_reference.heading: PIPEDA — Mandatory Breach Notification Requirements
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, ss. 10.1, 10.2 (as amended by Digital Privacy Act, S.C. 2015, c. 32)

tested_concepts: PIPEDA dual mandatory notification — Commissioner AND individuals simultaneously
Commissioner is not a gatekeeper for individual notification decisions
real risk of significant harm trigger — not limited to confirmed fraud
breach records must be maintained regardless of threshold

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: FIPPA Section 18 — Economic and Other Interests of Ontario
fact_pattern: A business association submits a FIPPA access request to the Ministry of Finance for internal economic analysis reports prepared by ministry economists projecting the fiscal impact of a proposed tax reform. The Ministry refuses access, arguing that disclosure of its economic forecasting methodology and projections would harm Ontario's ability to negotiate effectively with the federal government on equalization arrangements. The requester challenges the refusal before the IPC.
call_of_question: Under FIPPA, which exemption permits an institution to refuse disclosure of records where disclosure could reasonably be expected to prejudice the economic interests of Ontario or the competitive position of an Ontario institution?

options.A: Section 14 (law enforcement) — it applies to any government record whose disclosure could harm the province.
options.B: Section 19 (solicitor-client privilege) — it protects all internal government analysis, including economic forecasts.
options.C: Section 18(1) — a discretionary exemption permitting the institution to refuse disclosure of a record that could reasonably be expected to prejudice the economic interests or competitive position of an Ontario institution, or reveal information that could be exploited to the economic disadvantage of Ontario.
options.D: Section 21 (personal privacy) — it applies where the record contains information about identifiable individuals, including government economists.

explanation: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 18(1), an institution may refuse to disclose a record that contains: (a) trade secrets or financial, commercial, scientific, or technical information belonging to the Government of Ontario; (b) information where disclosure could reasonably be expected to prejudice the economic interests of an institution or the competitive position of an Ontario institution; or (c) information whose disclosure could be exploited to the economic disadvantage of Ontario. Section 18 is a discretionary exemption (the institution 'may' refuse); the institution must exercise its discretion and demonstrate that disclosure would cause the defined economic harm.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Section 14 is the law enforcement exemption and applies to records relating to law enforcement investigations, not to economic or fiscal analysis or intergovernmental negotiations.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. Section 19 (solicitor-client privilege) protects legal advice communications between lawyer and client; it does not apply to economic analysis reports prepared by ministry economists who are not providing legal advice.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 18(1) is a discretionary exemption permitting the institution to refuse disclosure of a record that could reasonably be expected to prejudice the economic interests or competitive position of an Ontario institution, or reveal information that could be exploited to the economic disadvantage of Ontario.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 21 (personal privacy) protects personal information about identifiable individuals; it does not apply to aggregate economic analysis or fiscal projections, which are institutional rather than personal information.

source_reference.heading: FIPPA Section 18 — Economic and Other Interests of Ontario
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 18(1)

tested_concepts: FIPPA s. 18 economic interests discretionary exemption
prejudice to economic interests or competitive position
distinction from ss. 14 19 21
